content.text: Temperature and climatic conditions vary greatly depending on geographical location. For example, Gangtok experiences very cold winters, while places like Kerala are comparatively warm and humid during winter. Kerala's warmer climate is due to its proximity to the equator and its long coastline.
The Sun is the main source of heat and light for the Earth, and the climate is generally hotter around regions closer to the equator.

content.title: Let Us Experiment — Heat Transfer in a Metal Strip
procedure: Attach four pins (I, II, III, IV) at equal distances on a 15 cm metal strip using wax.
Secure the strip horizontally and heat the free end (away from the stand) with a candle or spirit lamp.
Observe the order in which the pins fall.
content.observation: Pin I (closest to the flame) falls first, followed sequentially by pins II, III, and IV.
content.conclusion: Heat travels along the metal strip from the hotter end to the colder end. As heat reaches each pin, it melts the wax, causing the pin to fall. This process of heat transfer from a hotter part to a colder part in a solid is called conduction.

content.title: Process of Conduction
content.text: In the process of conduction, the particle that gets heated passes the heat on to its neighbour, and so on. Crucially, the particles themselves do NOT move away from their positions. Heat transfer in solids takes place mainly through conduction.

content.title: Good Conductors
content.text: Materials that allow heat to pass through them easily are called good conductors of heat. Metals (like steel, aluminium, iron) are good conductors. This is why cooking utensils, like a thukpa pan, are made of metals.

content.title: Poor Conductors (Insulators)
content.text: Materials that do not allow heat to pass through them easily are called poor conductors or insulators of heat. Examples include wood, glass, clay, porcelain, and air. Hot tea or coffee stays hot longer in clay or porcelain cups because they do not conduct heat away quickly.

content.title: Applications of Insulators in Daily Life
items: Woollen clothes trap air in their pores. Since air is a poor conductor of heat, it reduces heat flow from our body to the surroundings, keeping us warm.
Two thin blankets are warmer than one thick blanket because the layer of air trapped between the two thin blankets acts as an insulator.
The handles of cooking utensils are usually made of plastic or wood (insulators) so they can be held safely while hot.

content.title: Fascinating Facts — House Construction in Extreme Climates
content.text: In the upper Himalayas (e.g., Mori block in Uttarakhand), houses are built with double wooden walls filled with cow dung and mud. These materials act as poor conductors to prevent heat loss and keep interiors warm. Similarly, hollow bricks trap air (an insulator) inside them, keeping buildings warm in winter and cool in summer.

content.title: Let Us Investigate — Hot Air Rises
procedure: Hang two identical inverted paper cups on the ends of a horizontally balanced wooden stick.
Place a burning candle exactly below one of the cups and observe the balance.
content.observation: The cup placed above the candle flame rises up, tilting the balance.
content.conclusion: The candle heats the air below the cup. As air warms up, it expands, occupies more space, becomes lighter than the surrounding cooler air, and rises, pushing the cup upward.

content.title: Examples of Hot Air Rising
content.text:
(1) A partially inflated balloon placed in the Sun expands and grows larger as the trapped air heats up.
(2) Smoke from an incense stick (a mixture of hot gases and tiny solid particles) rises because it is warmer and lighter than the surrounding air.

content.title: Let Us Find Out — Convection in Water
procedure: Take a beaker half-filled with water and place a grain of potassium permanganate at the centre of its base.
Place a candle right below the centre of the base and observe the movement of the coloured streak.
content.observation: A streak of coloured water moves straight up from the centre, spreads out at the top, and then comes down along the sides of the beaker.
content.conclusion: Water at the bottom gets heated, expands, becomes lighter, and rises. Cooler, heavier water from the sides moves down to take its place, gets heated, and also rises. This cycle continues until the entire volume is heated.

content.title: Process of Convection
content.text: Convection is the process of heat transfer where the entire volume of a liquid or gas gets heated through the actual movement of its particles from one place to another. Unlike solids, liquids and gases primarily transfer heat via convection.

content.title: Let Us Investigate — Heating of Soil and Water
procedure: Take two identical bowls: fill one halfway with soil (land) and the other halfway with water (sea).
Fix a thermometer in each and place the setup in direct sunlight.
Measure the temperature every 5 minutes for 20 minutes, then bring them indoors to cool and measure again.
content.observation: The temperature of the soil rises much faster than that of the water. Upon cooling, the soil also cools down faster than the water.
content.conclusion: Land heats up faster and cools down faster than water.

content.title: Sea Breeze and Land Breeze Phenomena
items: Sea Breeze (Daytime): During the day, the land gets heated faster than the sea. The warm air above the land rises, and the cooler, heavier air from above the sea rushes in to take its place. This movement of cooler air from sea to land provides relief from heat in coastal areas. Windows in such areas face the sea to catch this breeze.

Land Breeze (Nighttime): At night, the land cools down much faster than the sea. The air above the sea remains warmer and rises up, causing cooler air from the land to move towards the sea. This movement is called the land breeze.

content.title: Process of Radiation
content.text: Radiation is the process of heat transfer that does not require any material medium. Heat from a fireplace directly reaches a person sitting nearby through radiation. Heat from the Sun reaches the Earth through the vacuum of space via radiation.
All objects radiate heat to their surroundings. For instance, a hot utensil removed from a flame gradually cools down because it radiates its heat into the surrounding environment.

content.title: Dive Deeper — Clothing Colors and Radiation
content.text: Light-coloured clothes reflect most of the heat that falls on them, making them comfortable to wear during hot summers. Dark-coloured surfaces absorb much more heat, making dark clothes preferable for keeping warm during cold winters.
Conduction, convection, and radiation often occur simultaneously in everyday life.

content.title: Example: Heating Water in a Pan
items: Conduction: Heat transfers from the flame to the metal pan.
Convection: The water inside the pan gets heated and circulates.
Radiation: The warmth felt by a person standing near the glowing flame and the hot pan is transferred via radiation.

content.title: Fascinating Facts — The Bukhari
content.text: In the Himalayan region, a traditional room heater called a bukhari is used. It is an iron stove burning wood or charcoal with a chimney pipe. When used to cook food on its flat top and warm a room, all three processes of heat transfer—conduction, convection, and radiation—are actively involved.

topics.topic_name: States of Water and the Environment

content.text: Water exists in three states: liquid (oceans, rivers, lakes), solid (snow, ice sheets, glaciers), and gas (water vapour in the atmosphere). The Sun's radiant heat melts mountain snow in summer, creating rivers that flow into oceans, which are then replenished by fresh snow in winter.

content.title: Stages of the Water Cycle
items: Evaporation & Transpiration: The Sun's heat causes water in oceans, lakes, and rivers to evaporate into water vapour. Plants also release water vapour through transpiration.
Condensation & Precipitation: Rising water vapour cools down and condenses to form clouds. These clouds eventually bring rain, snow, or hail back down to Earth in a process called precipitation.
Collection: The rainwater flows into water bodies or seeps into the ground.

content.title: Significance of the Water Cycle
content.text: The continuous upward and downward movement of water passing through soil, rocks, and plants is the water cycle. It helps redistribute, replenish, and conserve the total amount of water on Earth.

content.title: Know A Scientist — Varahamihira
content.text: Varahamihira was a 6th-century CE astronomer and mathematician in Ujjaini (Madhya Pradesh). In his work 'Brihatsamhita', he documented methods for predicting seasonal rainfall based on cloud formations, wind patterns, stars, and the moon.

topics.topic_name: 7.4.1 Seepage of water beneath the Earth

content.title: Let Us Investigate — Seepage of Water
procedure: Take three inverted cut plastic bottles with holes in their caps. Fill them separately with clay, sand, and gravel.
Place identical beakers under each bottle and pour 200 mL of water into each.
Collect and compare the outflowing water for 10 minutes.
content.observation: Water seeps fastest through the gravel, slower through the sand, and slowest through the clay.
content.conclusion: Because the empty spaces between gravel particles are wider, water seeps through them most easily. Water infiltrates more readily when the spaces between soil or rock particles are wide, open, and interconnected.

content.title: Groundwater Concepts
items: Infiltration: The process of surface rainwater seeping down through soil and rocks.
Groundwater: The seeping water that eventually gets stored in the pore spaces of underground sediments and rock openings.
Aquifers: The massive underground layers of sediments and rocks that store groundwater in their pore spaces. We extract this by digging wells or bore wells.

content.text: Excessive groundwater extraction for an increasing population, combined with heavy concrete urbanisation, has depleted groundwater levels. The water cycle, along with human interventions like rainwater harvesting pits, is essential for recharging these underground aquifers to ensure a sustainable water supply.

content.title: Science and Society — Ice Stupa
content.text: In Ladakh, streams dry up during spring because the Sun's heat isn't strong enough to melt mountain snow. During the freezing winter, locals pipe water downward and spray it into the air, where it instantly freezes layer by layer into a tall cone called an 'Ice Stupa'. When spring arrives, this massive ice cone melts slowly, providing a steady water supply for summer farming.

glossary.definition: The process of heat transfer from the hotter part of an object to a colder part, wherein particles pass the heat to their neighbours without moving from their positions (primarily occurs in solids).
Materials, such as metals, that allow heat to pass through them easily.
Materials, such as wood, glass, air, and plastic, that do not allow heat to pass through them easily. Also known as poor conductors.
The process of heat transfer in liquids and gases wherein the actual movement of heated, expanded, and lighter particles transfers heat throughout the fluid.
The process of heat transfer from a hotter body to a colder body that occurs directly without the requirement of any material medium (e.g., heat from the Sun).
The movement of cooler air from above the sea towards the land during the daytime, caused when the hot air over the rapidly heating land rises.
The movement of cooler air from above the land towards the sea during the nighttime, caused when the relatively warmer air over the sea rises.
The continuous movement of water from the Earth's surface to the atmosphere as vapour and back down as precipitation, passing through various environments.
The process by which condensed water vapour in clouds falls back to the Earth as rain, snow, or hail.
The process by which surface water (like rain) seeps vertically into the ground through the pores in soil and rocks.
Water that has seeped beneath the Earth's surface and is stored in the pore spaces of sediments and rock openings.
Underground layers of porous rock or sediments that store large quantities of groundwater, serving as sources for wells and bore wells.

summary_points: Heat is transferred fundamentally in three distinct ways: conduction, convection, and radiation.
Conduction predominantly occurs in solids, where heat energy passes from particle to particle without any actual displacement of the particles.
Materials are categorized as good conductors (metals) if they permit heat flow easily, or insulators (wood, air, plastic) if they restrict it.
Convection occurs in liquids and gases (fluids), where the heated, less dense fluid physically rises and cooler fluid sinks, creating continuous circulating currents.
Differences in heating and cooling rates between land and water give rise to sea breezes during the day and land breezes at night via convection.
Radiation transfers heat energy directly in the form of waves without needing any intervening material medium, enabling heat to travel from the Sun to Earth.
The water cycle involves evaporation (driven by the Sun's radiant heat), condensation, precipitation, and redistribution, sustaining the Earth's water balance.
Infiltration allows rainwater to seep through permeable soil and rocks and be stored underground as groundwater within vast aquifers.
